title: Bullpup

infobox.value: [firearm](/wiki/Firearm "Firearm") is one with its firing grip located in front of the [breech](/wiki/Chamber_(firearms) "Chamber (firearms)") of the weapon, instead of behind it.[[1]](#cite_note-1) This creates a weapon with a shorter overall length for a given barrel length, and one that is often lighter, more compact, concealable, and more maneuverable than a conventionally configured firearm. Where it is desirable for troops to be issued a more compact weapon, the use of a bullpup configuration allows for barrel length to be retained, thus preserving [muzzle velocity](/wiki/Muzzle_velocity "Muzzle velocity"), range, and ballistic effectiveness.[[2]](#cite_note-:0-2)

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Type of firearm configuration

Not to be confused with [Buhl Bull Pup](/wiki/Buhl_Bull_Pup "Buhl Bull Pup") or [AGM-12 Bullpup](/wiki/AGM-12_Bullpup "AGM-12 Bullpup").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9a/%D0%A1%D0%92%D0%A3-%D0%90%D0%A1.jpg/330px-%D0%A1%D0%92%D0%A3-%D0%90%D0%A1.jpg)](/wiki/File:%D0%A1%D0%92%D0%A3-%D0%90%D0%A1.jpg)

The [OTs-03 SVU](/wiki/Dragunov_SVU "Dragunov SVU"), a bullpup rifle with the grip and trigger located in front of the [action](/wiki/Action_(firearms) "Action (firearms)")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3a/SVD_rifle.jpg/330px-SVD_rifle.jpg)](/wiki/File:SVD_rifle.jpg)

The [SVDS](/wiki/Dragunov_sniper_rifle#Variants "Dragunov sniper rifle"), a conventionally configured rifle using the same action

A **bullpup** [firearm](/wiki/Firearm "Firearm") is one with its firing grip located in front of the [breech](/wiki/Chamber_(firearms) "Chamber (firearms)") of the weapon, instead of behind it.[[1]](#cite_note-1) This creates a weapon with a shorter overall length for a given barrel length, and one that is often lighter, more compact, concealable, and more maneuverable than a conventionally configured firearm. Where it is desirable for troops to be issued a more compact weapon, the use of a bullpup configuration allows for barrel length to be retained, thus preserving [muzzle velocity](/wiki/Muzzle_velocity "Muzzle velocity"), range, and ballistic effectiveness.[[2]](#cite_note-:0-2)

The bullpup concept was first tested militarily in 1901 with the British [Thorneycroft carbine](/wiki/Thorneycroft_carbine "Thorneycroft carbine"), but it was not until the [Cold War](/wiki/Cold_War "Cold War") that more successful designs and improvements led to wider adoption. In 1977, the [Austrian Army](/wiki/Austrian_Federal_Army "Austrian Federal Army") became the first military force in the world to adopt a bullpup rifle, the [Steyr AUG](/wiki/Steyr_AUG "Steyr AUG"), as a [principal combat weapon](/wiki/Service_rifle "Service rifle"). Since then the militaries in many countries have followed suit with other bullpup designs, such as the Chinese [QBZ-95](/wiki/QBZ-95 "QBZ-95"), Israeli [IWI Tavor](/wiki/IWI_Tavor "IWI Tavor"), French [FAMAS](/wiki/FAMAS "FAMAS") and British [SA80](/wiki/SA80 "SA80").

## Etymology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bullpup&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Etymology")]

The origin of
term "bullpup" for this configuration has long been unclear. In 1957, the word was reported to denote a target pistol, particularly one with a fancy stock.[[3]](#cite_note-DalzellVictor2014-3)

British firearm expert [Jonathan Ferguson](/wiki/Jonathan_Ferguson "Jonathan Ferguson") researched the origin of the term in 2019–2020. He found early references in 1930s firearm magazines implying that "bullpup" is derived from an [analogy](/wiki/Analogy "Analogy") of such rifles to [bulldog](/wiki/Bulldog "Bulldog") [puppies](/wiki/Puppies "Puppies") (colloquially called "bullpups" in England during the late 19th and the early 20th century), which were considered "squat, ugly but still aggressive and powerful".[[4]](#cite_note-4) The original meaning of the word to describe dogs has since fallen out of use.

## Description

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bullpup&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Description")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f3/FN_P90_PDW.jpg/250px-FN_P90_PDW.jpg)](/wiki/File:FN_P90_PDW.jpg)

The [FN P90](/wiki/FN_P90 "FN P90") uses the bullpup layout in conjunction with a unique top-mounted feeding system, making it the most compact [submachine gun](/wiki/Submachine_gun "Submachine gun") with a fixed stock.

The bullpup design places the gun's action mechanism and magazine behind the trigger, and the [receiver](/wiki/Receiver_(firearms) "Receiver (firearms)") functionally serves as the [buttstock](/wiki/Buttstock "Buttstock") with usually only a thin endplate,[[5]](#cite_note-Dockery2007-5) making the gun more of a "stockless" weapon from a pure technical sense. The [magazine](/wiki/Magazine_(firearms) "Magazine (firearms)") is also inserted behind the trigger group[[5]](#cite_note-Dockery2007-5) (technically it only needs the magazine's feeding slot to be located behind the trigger for the gun to be classified as a bullpup), but in some designs such as the [Heckler & Koch G11](/wiki/Heckler_%26_Koch_G11 "Heckler & Koch G11"), [FN P90](/wiki/FN_P90 "FN P90") and [Neostead](/wiki/Neostead "Neostead"), the magazine can extend forward beyond the trigger.[[6]](#cite_note-Cutshaw2011-6)

### Advantages

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bullpup&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Advantages")]

* The primary benefit of a bullpup weapon is that the weapon's overall length can be significantly decreased without reducing the barrel length. This allows a bullpup weapon to be more easily maneuvered and concealed than a conventional weapon with a similar barrel length, especially in tight spaces.[[7]](#cite_note-Tilstra2014-7)
* In some designs, the shorter length of stock reduces the weight compared to a conventional rifle with the same action.
* The [center of mass](/wiki/Center_of_mass "Center of mass") of a bullpup weapon is more posterior, thus closer to the shooter's [core](/wiki/Core_(anatomy) "Core (anatomy)"). This means less [torque](/wiki/Torque "Torque") when moved around, making the handling [kinematically](/wiki/Kinematic "Kinematic") more comfortable, especially when the shooter is running.
* Due to the shorter distance between the action and the butt plate, the [recoil](/wiki/Recoil "Recoil") impulse is transmitted more directly into the shooter's shoulder, with less [leverage](/wiki/Leverage_(mechanics) "Leverage (mechanics)") to create [muzzle rise](/wiki/Muzzle_rise "Muzzle rise").

### Disadvantages

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bullpup&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Disadvantages")]

* When using a bullpup weapon, the user's face is much closer to the action. This can increase noise issues and cause irritation to the shooter's eye and nose from exhaust gas. Spent [cartridge cases](/wiki/Cartridge_case "Cartridge case") may be ejected directly into the face of left-handed shooters. It can be difficult for a right-handed shooter to "off-hand" the weapon.[[5]](#cite_note-Dockery2007-5) For weapons with reciprocating charging handles, there is also the risk of their charging handles striking left-handed users. The process of changing the ejection side varies in complexity depending on the weapon, but as a general rule it requires at least some disassembly and cannot be done "on the fly", such as when an unusual shooting position might call for the weapon to be used on the other shoulder. On some weapons, such as the [SA80](/wiki/SA80 "SA80"), changing the ejection side is simply not possible. As a result, bullpups often require unusual ejection mechanisms to allow easy ambidextrous operation. This is solved on some designs with ejection downward ([FN P90](/wiki/FN_P90 "FN P90"), [Kel-Tec RDB](/wiki/Kel-Tec_RDB "Kel-Tec RDB")) or forward ([FN F2000](/wiki/FN_F2000 "FN F2000"),[[5]](#cite_note-Dockery2007-5) [Kel-Tec RFB](/wiki/Kel-Tec_RFB "Kel-Tec RFB")).[[8]](#cite_note-Sweeney2011-8)
* In case of a catastrophic failure, a bullpup weapon is more dangerous because both the barrel and the action are nearer to the shooter's head, neck and torso.
* When using [iron sights](/wiki/Iron_sights "Iron sights"), bullpup firearms typically have a shorter sight radius than conventional designs of the same barrel length, compromising accuracy.
* Bullpup weapons need a much longer trigger-[sear](/wiki/Sear_(firearm) "Sear (firearm)") [linkage](/wiki/Linkage_(mechanical) "Linkage (mechanical)") due to the trigger's forward position, so they tend to have stiffer, less precise triggers. Trigger pull characteristics are consequently a frequent criticism of bullpup weapons.[[9]](#cite_note-Sween2004-9)
* Changing magazines is often less intuitive and ergonomic,[[10]](#cite_note-10) and it is very difficult to perform a "drop free" change. This can be particularly problematic when the weapon is equipped with a [sling](/wiki/Sling_(firearms) "Sling (firearms)"), which attaches nearer to the magazine well in bullpup weapons and can physically interfere with magazine insertion.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bullpup&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: History")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bc/Thorneycroft_carbine%2C_patent_14622_of_July_18%2C_1901.png/330px-Thorneycroft_carbine%2C_patent_14622_of_July_18%2C_1901.png)](/wiki/File:Thorneycroft_carbine,_patent_14622_of_July_18,_1901.png)

[Thorneycroft carbine](/wiki/Thorneycroft_carbine "Thorneycroft carbine") patent

The earliest bullpup firearm known is a heavy [bench-rest](/wiki/Benchrest_shooting "Benchrest shooting") target rifle made circa 1860 for Richard Potter by Riviere of London. It weighs more than 6 kg (13 lb 4 oz) and features an octagonal barrel of approximately 20 bore (0.60 in) in calibre, with two-groove [Brunswick rifling](/wiki/Brunswick_rifle "Brunswick rifle"). It is held in the collection of the British [National Rifle Association](/wiki/National_Rifle_Association_(United_Kingdom)#Museum_of_the_National_Rifle_Association "National Rifle Association (United Kingdom)").[[11]](#cite_note-11) One of the earliest repeating bullpup designs was patented by William Joseph Curtis in 1866.[[12]](#cite_note-12) The concept was later used in [bolt-action](/wiki/Bolt-action "Bolt-action") rifles such as the [Thorneycroft carbine](/wiki/Thorneycroft_carbine "Thorneycroft carbine") of 1901, although the increased distance from hand grip to bolt handle meant the decreased length had to be weighed against the increased time required to fire. It was used in [semi-automatic firearms](/wiki/Semi-automatic_firearm "Semi-automatic firearm") in 1918 (6.5 mm French Faucon-Meunier [semi-automatic rifle](/wiki/Semi-automatic_rifle "Semi-automatic rifle") developed by Lt. Col. Armand-Frédéric Faucon), then in 1936 a bullpup machine pistol was patented by the Frenchman [Henri Delacre](/w/index.php?title=Henri_Delacre&action=edit&redlink=1 "Henri Delacre (page does not exist)").[[13]](#cite_note-Dugelby1984-13) The first bullpup design used in combat was the PzB M.SS.41 [anti-tank rifle](/wiki/Anti-tank_rifle "Anti-tank rifle") during World War II.[[14]](#cite_note-14) It was a Czech weapon used by the [SS](/wiki/Schutzstaffel "Schutzstaffel"), produced under German occupation.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/82/Enfield_bullpup_prototype.jpg/330px-Enfield_bullpup_prototype.jpg)](/wiki/File:Enfield_bullpup_prototype.jpg)

[EM-2](/wiki/EM-2_rifle "EM-2 rifle"), an experimental British assault rifle from the 1950s.

After World War II, Western engineers drew inspiration from the German [Sturmgewehr 44](/wiki/StG_44 "StG 44") [assault rifle](/wiki/Assault_rifle "Assault rifle"), which offered a compromise between bolt-action rifles and submachine guns. Among them was [Kazimierz Januszewski](/wiki/Kazimierz_Januszewski "Kazimierz Januszewski") (also known as Stefan Janson), a Polish engineer who had worked at the Polish national arsenal during the 1930s. After being mobilized during World War II he escaped German and Soviet forces and made his way to England, where he was a part of the "Polish design team" at [Enfield Lock](/wiki/Enfield_Lock "Enfield Lock")'s [Royal Small Arms Factory](/wiki/Royal_Small_Arms_Factory "Royal Small Arms Factory"). The factory was run by lieutenant colonel Edward Kent-Lemon. As Januszewski was developing a new rifle, the "Ideal Calibre Board" was searching for a replacement for the .303 cartridge. The Board decided on an optimal [7 mm](/wiki/.280_British ".280 British") cartridge on which Januszewski and the two teams working at Enfield had to base their designs. One design team led by Stanley Thorpe produced a gas-powered rifle with a locking system based on the Sturmgewehr. The design used steel pressings which were difficult to obtain, and so was scrapped. The result of the Polish design team's efforts was the [EM-2](/wiki/EM-2_rifle "EM-2 rifle"), which broke significant ground.[[15]](#cite_note-dugelby-15)

The EM-2 contained some similarities to the Soviet [AK-47](/wiki/AK-47 "AK-47"), although Januszewski had never seen the Soviet rifle. The first significant bullpup assault rifle came from the British programme to replace the service pistols, sub-machine guns, and rifles. In the two forms of the [EM-1](/wiki/EM-2_rifle#Background "EM-2 rifle") and the EM-2, the new rifle concept was born as a result of the experience with small arms that was gained during the Second World War.[[15]](#cite_note-dugelby-15)

It was obvious that modern warfare would require the infantry to be armed with a light, selective fire weapon, with effective range much longer than that of a submachine gun, but shorter than that of conventional semi-automatic or bolt-action rifles. The bullpup design was deemed necessary to retain the accuracy at range while reducing overall length. The EM-2 was adopted by the UK in 1951 as the world's first (limited) service bullpup rifle, but was promptly displaced by the adoption of the [7.62×51mm NATO](/wiki/7.62%C3%9751mm_NATO "7.62×51mm NATO") (0.308 in) cartridge, to which the EM-2 was not easily adapted. The decision was rescinded and a variant of the more conventional [FN FAL](/wiki/FN_FAL "FN FAL") was adopted in its place.[[16]](#cite_note-Cashner2013-16)

A [7.62×39mm M43](/wiki/7.62%C3%9739mm#M43 "7.62×39mm") calibre experimental assault rifle was developed by [German A. Korobov](/w/index.php?title=German_A._Korobov&action=edit&redlink=1 "German A. Korobov (page does not exist)") in the [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union") around 1945, and a further development, the [TKB-408](/wiki/TKB-408 "TKB-408") was entered for the 1946–47 assault rifle trials by the Soviet Army, although it was rejected in favour of the more conventional [AK-47](/wiki/AK-47 "AK-47"). The United States briefly experimented in the same year with the integrally-scoped [Model 45A](/wiki/Model_45A "Model 45A") bullpup, which never progressed beyond the prototype; [John Garand](/wiki/John_Garand "John Garand") designed the T31 bullpup, which was abandoned after his retirement in 1953.

After these failures of the bullpup design to achieve widespread service, the concept continued to be explored (for example: a second Korobov bullpup, the [TKB-022PM](/wiki/TKB-022PM "TKB-022PM")).

### Adoption

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bullpup&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Adoption")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/39/AUG_A1_508mm_04.jpg/250px-AUG_A1_508mm_04.jpg)](/wiki/File:AUG_A1_508mm_04.jpg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d7/IWI-Tavor-TAR-21w1.jpg/250px-IWI-Tavor-TAR-21w1.jpg)](/wiki/File:IWI-Tavor-TAR-21w1.jpg)

The [QBZ-95](/wiki/QBZ-95 "QBZ-95") is one of the [most-produced firearms](/wiki/List_of_most-produced_firearms "List of most-produced firearms") in the world with approximately 3 million weapons made.

[IPI Malyuk](/wiki/Malyuk "Malyuk")

The [Steyr AUG](/wiki/Steyr_AUG "Steyr AUG") (selected in 1977) is often cited as the first successful bullpup,[[17]](#cite_note-17)[[18]](#cite_note-18)[[19]](#cite_note-Lewis2011-19) in service with the armed forces of over twenty countries, and the primary rifle of Austria and Australia. It was highly advanced for the 1970s, combining in the same weapon the bullpup configuration, extensive use of polymer, dual vertical grips, an optical sight as standard, and a modular design. Highly reliable, light, and accurate, the Steyr AUG showed clearly the potential of the bullpup layout. The arrival of the [FAMAS](/wiki/FAMAS "FAMAS") in 1978, and its adoption by France emphasized the slide from traditional to bullpup layouts within rifle designs.[[19]](#cite_note-Lewis2011-19)

The British resumed their bullpup experiments with the [L85](/wiki/SA80 "SA80"), which entered service in 1985. After persistent reliability problems, it was redesigned by the then British-owned [Heckler & Koch](/wiki/Heckler_%26_Koch "Heckler & Koch") into the [L85A2](/wiki/SA80#Rifle "SA80"), to be a fully reliable weapon.[[20]](#cite_note-Hogg2003-20) As of 2016, it was replaced by the [L85A3](/wiki/SA80#Rifle "SA80") which is lighter, more adaptable, and more durable.

Having learned from extensive combat experience, [Israel Military Industries](/wiki/Israel_Military_Industries "Israel Military Industries") developed a bullpup rifle: the [Tavor TAR-21](/wiki/IMI_Tavor_TAR-21 "IMI Tavor TAR-21"). The Tavor is light, accurate, fully ambidextrous and reliable (designed to stringent reliability standards to avoid malfunctioning in desert conditions), and is in increasing demand in other countries, notably India.[[5]](#cite_note-Dockery2007-5) The Tavor shares many similarities with the [SAR 21](/wiki/SAR_21 "SAR 21") and the South African [Vektor CR-21](/wiki/Vektor_CR-21 "Vektor CR-21").[[5]](#cite_note-Dockery2007-5)

The Chinese [People's Liberation Army](/wiki/People%27s_Liberation_Army "People's Liberation Army") adopted the [Type 95 gun family](/wiki/QBZ-95#Variants "QBZ-95") in 1997, a family of bullpup firearms sharing a common receiver design, which includes the [QBZ-95](/wiki/QBZ-95 "QBZ-95") standard rifle, a carbine and [light support weapon](/wiki/Light_support_weapon "Light support weapon") variants. The [Islamic Republic of Iran Army](/wiki/Islamic_Republic_of_Iran_Army "Islamic Republic of Iran Army") has adopted the [KH-2002](/wiki/KH-2002 "KH-2002") in limited numbers.[[21]](#cite_note-Lai2016-21)

Some sniper rifles such as the American [Barrett M95](/wiki/Barrett_M95 "Barrett M95") and [XM500](/wiki/Barrett_XM500 "Barrett XM500"),[[22]](#cite_note-McNab2016-22) German [Walther WA 2000](/wiki/Walther_WA_2000 "Walther WA 2000") and [DSR-1](/wiki/DSR-1 "DSR-1"),[[23]](#cite_note-Dougherty2012-23) Chinese [QBU-88](/wiki/QBU-88 "QBU-88"), Russian [SVU](/wiki/Dragunov_SVU "Dragunov SVU"), Polish [Bor](/wiki/Bor_rifle "Bor rifle"). It is also used for [combat shotgun](/wiki/Combat_shotgun "Combat shotgun") designs such as the [Neostead](/wiki/Neostead "Neostead") and [Kel-Tec KSG](/wiki/Kel-Tec_KSG "Kel-Tec KSG").[[24]](#cite_note-MannLee2013-24)
